name: Taweret
description: Taweret, also spelled Taueret, Taurt, Tuat, and Tawaret, is an ancient Egyptian protective deity. She is typically depicted as a bipedal female hippopotamus with feline paws, the tail of a crocodile, and human breasts. This fearsome yet maternal appearance was meant to ward off evil spirits and protect women during pregnancy and childbirth. Taweret's hybrid form embodies the protective nature of dangerous animals, harnessing their power to safeguard the vulnerable. She was particularly revered as a patron of fertility and childbirth, ensuring the safe delivery of babies and the well-being of mothers. As a household deity, Taweret's image was frequently found on amulets, furniture, and other domestic objects, signifying her constant presence and watchful eye. Her association with the afterlife is also evident, as she was believed to assist in the regeneration of the deceased. Taweret's power stemmed from her ability to confront and overcome chaos, providing comfort and security in a world filled with uncertainty. She was a powerful symbol of protection, motherhood, and regeneration, embodying the fierce yet nurturing aspects of the divine feminine. Her influence extended from the daily lives of ordinary Egyptians to the grand narratives of the afterlife, solidifying her place as a beloved and respected deity.
subtitle: Protector of women and childbirth

keyMyths.title: Taweret's Protection
keyMyths.description: Taweret is believed to fiercely protect pregnant women and newborns from evil spirits that seek to harm them. Her image on amulets provides constant protection.

keyMyths.title: Taweret and Regeneration
keyMyths.description: In the afterlife, Taweret assists in the regeneration of the deceased, ensuring their safe passage into the next world. She helps overcome obstacles in the underworld.

keyMyths.title: Taweret and Bes
keyMyths.description: Taweret is often associated with Bes, another protector deity, and they are frequently depicted together, amplifying their protective powers. Together they ensure safety and prosperity.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson

sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Egyptian deities.
